title: Acute Cholangitis
oneLiner: Ascending bacterial infection of obstructed bile ducts; a biliary emergency with sepsis that demands urgent drainage to avoid mortality.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 72-year-old woman with known gallstones presents with 48 hours of right upper quadrant pain, rigors, and now looks jaundiced. On the ward she is febrile (38.9°C), tachycardic, and her BP is 95/60. She is alert but drowsy. Abdominal exam reveals RUQ tenderness without peritonism. Bloods show a white cell count of 18, CRP 220, bilirubin 85, ALP 650, with a raised procalcitonin.

**Dissect the vignette – pattern clues:**

| Clue | What it tells you |
|------|-------------------|
| Elderly, known gallstones | High pre-test probability for choledocholithiasis as obstructing cause |
| RUQ pain + fever + jaundice | **Charcot’s triad** – 75% sensitive for cholangitis, specific enough to start treatment |
| Rigors | Bacteraemia – often Gram-negative rods from bile |
| Tachycardia + borderline hypotension | Early distributive (septic) shock – the critical transition |
| Drowsiness (confusion) | **Reynolds’ pentad** – septic shock with CNS involvement; marks severe cholangitis |
| RUQ tenderness, no rebound | No frank peritonitis yet – gallbladder not perforated; pain from ductal distension |
| Marked cholestatic LFTs (↑ALP, ↑GGT, ↑bilirubin) | Obstructive pattern – bile duct block, not just hepatitis |
| Raised procalcitonin | Supports bacterial aetiology, helps distinguish from simple stone obstruction without infection |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Painless cholangitis** – in elderly or diabetics, the classic RUQ pain may be absent; they present only with fever, confusion, and hypotension. Miss it and you’ll label it “sepsis of unknown origin”. **Always image the biliary tree in elderly sepsis.**
- **Post-ERCP cholangitis** – happens <24h after a biliary intervention; pain may be masked by sedation or analgesia. Suspect if a patient who had ERCP suddenly spikes fever, develops rigors, or the bilirubin fails to fall.
- **Obstructive jaundice without initial fever** – early infection may be subclinical, especially with slow-growing organisms (e.g., *Enterococcus*). Fever may appear only after a few days. If jaundice and LFTs don’t improve after stone removal, consider persistent infection or stent occlusion.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Acute cholangitis is an infection of the bile ducts that occurs when bacteria from the duodenum ascend into a static pool of bile, usually because a stone, stricture, or tumour is blocking the outflow. The infected bile under pressure then drives bacteria and endotoxins into the bloodstream.

**Why it matters – frequency, stakes, pitfalls:**

- **Frequency:** 10–20% of patients with symptomatic gallstones will eventually get cholangitis. It is the second most common cause of sepsis in the elderly after UTIs.
- **Stakes:** Mortality of severe cholangitis treated only with antibiotics is ~40% – but with timely biliary drainage, it falls to <5%. **Every hour of delay increases the risk of multi-organ failure.**
- **Common pitfalls:** Junior doctors often focus on “working up the jaundice” with imaging and forget to start antibiotics immediately. Or they treat the UTI-like sepsis without imaging the biliary tree. Or they give antibiotics but do not push for urgent ERCP when the patient is stabilised – the infection will recur as long as the obstruction persists.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story:**
Normal bile is sterile and flows under low pressure (~5-10 cmH₂O) from the liver to the duodenum. A stone, stricture, or mass creates a partial or complete obstruction → bile flow slows and stagnates → bacteria from the duodenum (mostly *E. coli*, *Klebsiella*, *Enterococcus*) ascend via the common channel into the static bile and multiply. As pressure builds above the obstruction, the duct dilates. When pressure exceeds ~20 cmH₂O → **cholangiovenous reflux** – bacteria and endotoxins are forced through the disrupted tight junctions of the ductal wall directly into the hepatic sinusoids and systemic circulation.

**Mechanism → clinical sign:**

| Pathophysiological event | Resulting sign |
|--------------------------|----------------|
| Biliary obstruction | Jaundice, dark urine, pale stools; ↑ bilirubin, ALP, GGT |
| Ductal distension | RUQ pain (visceral, dull) ± radiation to back/right shoulder |
| Bacterial overgrowth + endotoxemia | Fever, rigors, tachycardia, ↑ WBC, procalcitonin |
| Cholangiovenous reflux | Bacteraemia → SIRS → septic shock (hypotension, confusion, AKI) |
| Endotoxins → vascular leak | Pulmonary oedema, worsening shock |
| Persistent obstruction + infection → hepatic microabscesses | Rising transaminases (hepatocellular injury), tender hepatomegaly |

**Connect back to pattern recognition:**  
The Charcot triad is the direct translation of the three pathophysiological steps: obstruction (jaundice) → distension (pain) → infection (fever). The Reynolds pentad signals that pressure has reached the threshold for cholangiovenous reflux → systemic endotoxemia with CNS depression and cardiovascular collapse.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Common examples | Stem clue that points to it |
|----------|----------------|----------------------------|
| **Gallstone disease** | Choledocholithiasis (most common cause) – a stone that has migrated from the gallbladder into the common bile duct | "Known gallstones" + recent RUQ colic |
| **Benign strictures** | Post-cholecystectomy, primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC), recurrent pyogenic cholangitis (Asia) | "Biliary surgery in the past" or "recurrent episodes" |
| **Malignant strictures** | Pancreatic head cancer, cholangiocarcinoma, ampullary tumour | "Painless jaundice", weight loss, palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier sign) |
| **Iatrogenic** | Post-ERCP, blocked biliary stent, after biliary surgery | "Symptoms started 6–12h after ERCP" or "known biliary stent" |
| **Parasitic** | Ascaris lumbricoides, liver flukes (Clonorchis, Opisthorchis) | "Immigrant from endemic area" + eosinophilia |
| **Rare causes** | Mirizzi syndrome (stone in gallbladder neck compressing the common hepatic duct), biliary sludge in critically ill, cystic fibrosis | "Recurrent jaundice with normal gallbladder" or "long ICU stay" |

**Highlight:** **Choledocholithiasis** accounts for >60% of all cases in Western populations. Always start your workup looking for a stone in the common bile duct.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:**
The **Tokyo Guidelines 2018 (TG18)** are the most widely used. A diagnosis of acute cholangitis requires **≥1 item from A, B, and C**:

| Domain | Items |
|--------|-------|
| **A: Systemic inflammation** | Fever ≥38°C / rigors / WBC <4 or >10 / CRP ≥10 mg/L |
| **B: Cholestasis** | Jaundice (bilirubin ≥34 μmol/L) / abnormal LFTs: ALP, GGT, ALT, AST >1.5x ULN |
| **C: Imaging** | Biliary dilatation / evidence of cause on imaging (stone, stricture, stent) |

Pattern recognition: If a patient has fever + obstructive LFTs + dilated CBD on ultrasound → **diagnose acute cholangitis until proven otherwise**. You do not need Charcot’s triad (all three).

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **LFTs** | Obstructive pattern: ↑bilirubin, ↑ALP, ↑GGT; ALT/AST may rise mildly (hepatocyte damage from pressure) | Screen for cholestasis; trend monitors drainage success | Normal LFTs make bile duct obstruction unlikely but don’t exclude early/cholangitis |
| **CRP + procalcitonin** | Acute phase reactants | Differentiate bacterial from viral/non-infective jaundice | PCT >0.5 ng/mL strongly suggests bacterial infection |
| **Full blood count** | WBC left shift, thrombocytopenia maybe | Baseline for sepsis severity | WBC >12 typical; <4 in severe sepsis (bad sign) |
| **Blood cultures** | Identify organism (70% Gram-negative, 20% Gram-positive, 10% anaerobes) | Guide switch to targeted antibiotics | 2 sets before antibiotics – positive in ~60% cases |
| **RUQ ultrasound** | Dilated CBD (>6-7 mm age-dependent), gallstones, gallbladder wall thickness | First-line imaging – assess for obstruction and alternative diagnoses (cholecystitis, abscess) | A dilated CBD + stone = obstructive cause; no dilatation may still happen in early/fitful obstruction |
| **MRCP** | Detailed anatomy of bile ducts, exact level of obstruction, stone presence | Best non-invasive confirmation; avoids ERCP in low-risk patients | High sensitivity (~95%) for choledocholithiasis |
| **ERCP** | Direct visualisation, therapeutic drainage, pus in bile | Gold standard – diagnostic and therapeutic in one; indicated in moderate/severe cholangitis | **Single finding that confirms diagnosis**: **pus seen emerging from the ampulla** |

**Pattern recognition – what single finding rules out?**  
A normal ultrasound (no dilated ducts, no stones) plus a normal MRCP makes cholangitis highly unlikely – but do not rely solely on normal LFTs. Conversely, **purulent bile at ERCP is 100% specific** for acute cholangitis (though not always positive).

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – "ABC + Abx + Decision"**

- **A/B/C:** Give high-flow oxygen if oxygen sat <94%; gain IV access x2; start IV fluids (500 mL-1L crystalloid bolus, re-assess response).
- **Blood cultures** before antibiotics – but do not delay antibiotics >30 min.
- **Antibiotics:** Empirical broad-spectrum covering Gram-negative, Gram-positive, and anaerobes.

| Regimen | Dose + Route | Duration |
|---------|--------------|----------|
| **Piperacillin-tazobactam** (best single agent) | 4.5 g IV q6h | 4-7 days post-drainage |
| **Ceftriaxone + metronidazole** (if pen allergic) | Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily + Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h | Same |
| **Meropenem** (for severe/high-resistance) | 1g IV q8h | Tailor to cultures |

- **Decision:** Call senior / gastro / interventional radiology to arrange **urgent biliary drainage** (ideally ERCP within 24 hours). For severe cholangitis (shock, confusion, high lactate), aim for <12 hours.
- **What NOT to do:** Do not just "monitor" or "wait for morning" – obstruction must be relieved. Do not give only antibiotics and discharge.

**Ward (24-72h) – "Drain, Monitor, Source Control"**

- **ERCP** with sphincterotomy, stone extraction, or stent placement. If ERCP impossible (altered anatomy, coagulopathy) → **percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage (PTBD)** by interventional radiology.
- **Monitor:** NEWS2 q1h until stable, then q4h. Daily LFTs, FBC, CRP. Watch for post-procedure pancreatitis, bleeding, perforation.
- **Adjust antibiotics** based on bile culture results (when available, 48-72h). De-escalate if possible.
- **Nutrition:** Nil by mouth until drainage established. Then advance as tolerated – no low-fat diet evidence; just avoid further obstruction.

**Long-term – "Prevent Recurrence"**

- **Cholecystectomy** – for gallbladder-in-situ patients, perform laparoscopic cholecystectomy after recovery (typically 4-6 weeks post-drainage). Do not do it during acute episode (higher risk of conversion, infection).
- **Treat underlying cause:** If stricture – consider balloon dilation, stent exchange. If malignant – refer to hepatobiliary oncology.
- **Consider ursodeoxycholic acid** for recurrent cholangitis in PSC (off-label use, no strong evidence).
- **What NOT to do:** Don’t forget to schedule the cholecystectomy – recurrence rate without it is 30-50% at 1 year.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Acute Cholangitis | Acute Cholecystitis | Acute Pancreatitis | Hepatic Abscess |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Pain** | RUQ, dull, constant ± back | RUQ, crescendo, may radiate to right shoulder | Epigastric, bores through to back | RUQ, constant, may be pleuritic |
| **Exam** | RUQ tenderness, no guarding unless empyema | Murphy’s sign (inspiratory arrest), mass | Epigastric guarding, Grey Turner/Cullen (late) | Hepatomegaly, tender liver |
| **Jaundice** | Present in >80% (obstructive) | Variable (mild in ~10% if Mirizzi) | Early mild jaundice, check direct vs indirect | Uncommon unless abscess compresses ducts |
| **Fever** | High, with rigors | Low-grade, no rigors usually | Mild/no fever unless infected necrosis | High, swinging |
| **LFTs** | Obstructive ↑ALP, ↑GGT, bilirubin; ALT mildly ↑ | Normal or mild ↑ALT | Moderate ↑AST/ALT (2-5x) + ↑lipase >3x ULN | Cholestatic if adjacent bile ducts compressed |
| **Key imaging** | Dilated bile duct, stone/stricture | Thickened gallbladder wall, pericholecystic fluid | Oedematous pancreas, necrosis | Single/multiple fluid collections ± gas in abscess |
| **Key differ** | Pus in bile at ERCP | Positive Murphy sign + GB wall thickening | Lipase >3x ULN | CT/MRI shows abscess cavity |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Septic shock** | Hypotension resistant to fluids, increasing lactate >2, altered GCS | NEWS2 ≥7, lactate >4, oliguria | Escalate to ICU, start vasopressors (norepinephrine), goal MAP ≥65, reassess for urgent drainage within hours |
| **Biliary peritonitis / gallbladder empyema** | RUQ rigidity, guarding, rebound; fever high | Tender mass, positive psoas sign | Urgent CT, emergency drainage (ERCP or PTBD) + surgery if perforated |
| **Liver abscess** | High swinging fevers, right upper quadrant pain, referred pain to shoulder | Hepatomegaly, ↑ALP, iron deficiency anaemia | Long course antibiotics (4-6 weeks), percutaneous drainage if >5 cm |
| **Acute kidney injury** | Oliguria, rising creatinine | Sepsis-induced hypoperfusion + nephrotoxic drugs | Fluids, avoid NSAIDs, remove obstructive cause |
| **Pancreatitis** | Epigastric pain, vomiting, elevated amylase/lipase | Ductal obstruction (e.g., stone in ampulla) | Endoscopic stone extraction, supportive care |
| **Late: Secondary biliary cirrhosis** | Insidious: ascites, splenomegaly, coagulopathy from chronic obstruction | Prolonged untreated obstruction (e.g., benign stricture) | Surgical drainage, liver transplant assessment |

**Late-presentation pattern – "the neglected cholangitis":**  
A patient presents with ascites, jaundice, and a rock-hard liver; no fever. They were jaundiced for months after a cholecystectomy and never followed up. The "missed" complication is **secondary biliary cirrhosis** from a retained stricture – irreversible unless caught early.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **Charcot’s Triad:** *“Pain, Fever, Jaundice”* – remember that you only need two for clinical suspicion.
2. **Reynolds’ Pentad:** *“Charcot + Shock + Confusion”* – for the septic variant needing immediate ICU.
3. **TG18 Criteria:** *“C-holestasis + I-nflammation + I-maging”* – (C+I+I) = diagnosis.

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

- **“Pus under pressure”** – the core concept. If you drain it, they get better; if you don't, they die. Antibiotics alone are inadequate.
- **Post-cholecystectomy sepsis** – always think cholangitis from a retained stone. Get an MRCP.
- **Bilirubin >50 μmol/L + dilated CBD >10mm** = almost certainly an obstructing stone. No further delays – book ERCP.
- **Fever + RUQ pain + normal ultrasound** – consider early infection without duct dilatation (e.g., segmental cholangitis in PSC). Still need MRCP/ERCP.
- **Monitor for post-ERCP pancreatitis** – especially in young female after sphincterotomy. If pain recurs within 6h, check amylase.
- **Blood cultures positive for *E. coli* or *Klebsiella* + hyperbilirubinaemia** – this is cholangitis until proven otherwise.
- **Never forget the gallbladder** – even after a successful ERCP, if the gallbladder is still in situ, plan cholecystectomy within 6 weeks to prevent recurrent obstruction.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**

| Mistake | Correct approach |
|---------|------------------|
| Starting antibiotics but not arranging drainage | Drainage is definitive therapy – antibiotics buy time |
| Using only Gram-negative coverage (e.g., ciprofloxacin alone) | Need Gram-negative, Gram-positive (enterococcus), and anaerobic coverage |
| Failing to get blood cultures before antibiotics | 60% yield; if negative, you can't narrow therapy |
| Doing a cholecystectomy during acute cholangitis | Wait 4-6 weeks – high risk of complications |

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call your senior (registrar / on-call consultant):**

- Any patient with **Charcot’s triad** (even without shock) – needs drainage decision.
- **NEWS2 ≥5** (any single orange score OR sum total).
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** – start early goal-directed therapy.
- **GCS <15** (particularly drowsiness or confusion) – indicates severe cholangitis.
- **Systolic BP <90 mmHg** despite 1L crystalloid – suspect septic shock.
- **Oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/h for >2h)** – risk of AKI.

**When to call ICU / crash team:**

- **NEWS2 ≥7** (or any red score as per local policy).
- **Requiring vasopressors** (IV fluids insufficient to maintain MAP ≥65).
- **GCS <9** or new focal neurology.
- **Rising lactate >4 mmol/L** despite initial resuscitation.
- **Progressive respiratory failure** (RR >30, PaO2 <8 kPa on O2).
- **Septic shock with multi-organ failure** (renal, hepatic, respiratory).

**Specific thresholds – use NEWS2 as your safety net:**
- NEWS2 0-4: Stable, plan ward management
- NEWS2 5-6: Immediate senior review, consider HDU/step-up
- NEWS2 ≥7: **Crash call / ICU outreach** – this patient requires organ support

**Bottom line:** If you even suspect severe acute cholangitis, **call early**, **start antibiotics**, **arrange drainage**. Do not watch a septic biliary patient deteriorate overnight. The one-shot teaching: **"Pus under pressure kills fast – drain it to save them."**

rawMarkdown:
## Acute Cholangitis
**One-liner:** Ascending bacterial infection of obstructed bile ducts; a biliary emergency with sepsis that demands urgent drainage to avoid mortality.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 72-year-old woman with known gallstones presents with 48 hours of right upper quadrant pain, rigors, and now looks jaundiced. On the ward she is febrile (38.9°C), tachycardic, and her BP is 95/60. She is alert but drowsy. Abdominal exam reveals RUQ tenderness without peritonism. Bloods show a white cell count of 18, CRP 220, bilirubin 85, ALP 650, with a raised procalcitonin.

**Dissect the vignette – pattern clues:**

| Clue | What it tells you |
|------|-------------------|
| Elderly, known gallstones | High pre-test probability for choledocholithiasis as obstructing cause |
| RUQ pain + fever + jaundice | **Charcot’s triad** – 75% sensitive for cholangitis, specific enough to start treatment |
| Rigors | Bacteraemia – often Gram-negative rods from bile |
| Tachycardia + borderline hypotension | Early distributive (septic) shock – the critical transition |
| Drowsiness (confusion) | **Reynolds’ pentad** – septic shock with CNS involvement; marks severe cholangitis |
| RUQ tenderness, no rebound | No frank peritonitis yet – gallbladder not perforated; pain from ductal distension |
| Marked cholestatic LFTs (↑ALP, ↑GGT, ↑bilirubin) | Obstructive pattern – bile duct block, not just hepatitis |
| Raised procalcitonin | Supports bacterial aetiology, helps distinguish from simple stone obstruction without infection |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Painless cholangitis** – in elderly or diabetics, the classic RUQ pain may be absent; they present only with fever, confusion, and hypotension. Miss it and you’ll label it “sepsis of unknown origin”. **Always image the biliary tree in elderly sepsis.**
- **Post-ERCP cholangitis** – happens <24h after a biliary intervention; pain may be masked by sedation or analgesia. Suspect if a patient who had ERCP suddenly spikes fever, develops rigors, or the bilirubin fails to fall.
- **Obstructive jaundice without initial fever** – early infection may be subclinical, especially with slow-growing organisms (e.g., *Enterococcus*). Fever may appear only after a few days. If jaundice and LFTs don’t improve after stone removal, consider persistent infection or stent occlusion.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
Acute cholangitis is an infection of the bile ducts that occurs when bacteria from the duodenum ascend into a static pool of bile, usually because a stone, stricture, or tumour is blocking the outflow. The infected bile under pressure then drives bacteria and endotoxins into the bloodstream.

**Why it matters – frequency, stakes, pitfalls:**

- **Frequency:** 10–20% of patients with symptomatic gallstones will eventually get cholangitis. It is the second most common cause of sepsis in the elderly after UTIs.
- **Stakes:** Mortality of severe cholangitis treated only with antibiotics is ~40% – but with timely biliary drainage, it falls to <5%. **Every hour of delay increases the risk of multi-organ failure.**
- **Common pitfalls:** Junior doctors often focus on “working up the jaundice” with imaging and forget to start antibiotics immediately. Or they treat the UTI-like sepsis without imaging the biliary tree. Or they give antibiotics but do not push for urgent ERCP when the patient is stabilised – the infection will recur as long as the obstruction persists.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story:**
Normal bile is sterile and flows under low pressure (~5-10 cmH₂O) from the liver to the duodenum. A stone, stricture, or mass creates a partial or complete obstruction → bile flow slows and stagnates → bacteria from the duodenum (mostly *E. coli*, *Klebsiella*, *Enterococcus*) ascend via the common channel into the static bile and multiply. As pressure builds above the obstruction, the duct dilates. When pressure exceeds ~20 cmH₂O → **cholangiovenous reflux** – bacteria and endotoxins are forced through the disrupted tight junctions of the ductal wall directly into the hepatic sinusoids and systemic circulation.

**Mechanism → clinical sign:**

| Pathophysiological event | Resulting sign |
|--------------------------|----------------|
| Biliary obstruction | Jaundice, dark urine, pale stools; ↑ bilirubin, ALP, GGT |
| Ductal distension | RUQ pain (visceral, dull) ± radiation to back/right shoulder |
| Bacterial overgrowth + endotoxemia | Fever, rigors, tachycardia, ↑ WBC, procalcitonin |
| Cholangiovenous reflux | Bacteraemia → SIRS → septic shock (hypotension, confusion, AKI) |
| Endotoxins → vascular leak | Pulmonary oedema, worsening shock |
| Persistent obstruction + infection → hepatic microabscesses | Rising transaminases (hepatocellular injury), tender hepatomegaly |

**Connect back to pattern recognition:**  
The Charcot triad is the direct translation of the three pathophysiological steps: obstruction (jaundice) → distension (pain) → infection (fever). The Reynolds pentad signals that pressure has reached the threshold for cholangiovenous reflux → systemic endotoxemia with CNS depression and cardiovascular collapse.

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Common examples | Stem clue that points to it |
|----------|----------------|----------------------------|
| **Gallstone disease** | Choledocholithiasis (most common cause) – a stone that has migrated from the gallbladder into the common bile duct | "Known gallstones" + recent RUQ colic |
| **Benign strictures** | Post-cholecystectomy, primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC), recurrent pyogenic cholangitis (Asia) | "Biliary surgery in the past" or "recurrent episodes" |
| **Malignant strictures** | Pancreatic head cancer, cholangiocarcinoma, ampullary tumour | "Painless jaundice", weight loss, palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier sign) |
| **Iatrogenic** | Post-ERCP, blocked biliary stent, after biliary surgery | "Symptoms started 6–12h after ERCP" or "known biliary stent" |
| **Parasitic** | Ascaris lumbricoides, liver flukes (Clonorchis, Opisthorchis) | "Immigrant from endemic area" + eosinophilia |
| **Rare causes** | Mirizzi syndrome (stone in gallbladder neck compressing the common hepatic duct), biliary sludge in critically ill, cystic fibrosis | "Recurrent jaundice with normal gallbladder" or "long ICU stay" |

**Highlight:** **Choledocholithiasis** accounts for >60% of all cases in Western populations. Always start your workup looking for a stone in the common bile duct.

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:**
The **Tokyo Guidelines 2018 (TG18)** are the most widely used. A diagnosis of acute cholangitis requires **≥1 item from A, B, and C**:

| Domain | Items |
|--------|-------|
| **A: Systemic inflammation** | Fever ≥38°C / rigors / WBC <4 or >10 / CRP ≥10 mg/L |
| **B: Cholestasis** | Jaundice (bilirubin ≥34 μmol/L) / abnormal LFTs: ALP, GGT, ALT, AST >1.5x ULN |
| **C: Imaging** | Biliary dilatation / evidence of cause on imaging (stone, stricture, stent) |

Pattern recognition: If a patient has fever + obstructive LFTs + dilated CBD on ultrasound → **diagnose acute cholangitis until proven otherwise**. You do not need Charcot’s triad (all three).

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **LFTs** | Obstructive pattern: ↑bilirubin, ↑ALP, ↑GGT; ALT/AST may rise mildly (hepatocyte damage from pressure) | Screen for cholestasis; trend monitors drainage success | Normal LFTs make bile duct obstruction unlikely but don’t exclude early/cholangitis |
| **CRP + procalcitonin** | Acute phase reactants | Differentiate bacterial from viral/non-infective jaundice | PCT >0.5 ng/mL strongly suggests bacterial infection |
| **Full blood count** | WBC left shift, thrombocytopenia maybe | Baseline for sepsis severity | WBC >12 typical; <4 in severe sepsis (bad sign) |
| **Blood cultures** | Identify organism (70% Gram-negative, 20% Gram-positive, 10% anaerobes) | Guide switch to targeted antibiotics | 2 sets before antibiotics – positive in ~60% cases |
| **RUQ ultrasound** | Dilated CBD (>6-7 mm age-dependent), gallstones, gallbladder wall thickness | First-line imaging – assess for obstruction and alternative diagnoses (cholecystitis, abscess) | A dilated CBD + stone = obstructive cause; no dilatation may still happen in early/fitful obstruction |
| **MRCP** | Detailed anatomy of bile ducts, exact level of obstruction, stone presence | Best non-invasive confirmation; avoids ERCP in low-risk patients | High sensitivity (~95%) for choledocholithiasis |
| **ERCP** | Direct visualisation, therapeutic drainage, pus in bile | Gold standard – diagnostic and therapeutic in one; indicated in moderate/severe cholangitis | **Single finding that confirms diagnosis**: **pus seen emerging from the ampulla** |

**Pattern recognition – what single finding rules out?**  
A normal ultrasound (no dilated ducts, no stones) plus a normal MRCP makes cholangitis highly unlikely – but do not rely solely on normal LFTs. Conversely, **purulent bile at ERCP is 100% specific** for acute cholangitis (though not always positive).

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – "ABC + Abx + Decision"**

- **A/B/C:** Give high-flow oxygen if oxygen sat <94%; gain IV access x2; start IV fluids (500 mL-1L crystalloid bolus, re-assess response).
- **Blood cultures** before antibiotics – but do not delay antibiotics >30 min.
- **Antibiotics:** Empirical broad-spectrum covering Gram-negative, Gram-positive, and anaerobes.

| Regimen | Dose + Route | Duration |
|---------|--------------|----------|
| **Piperacillin-tazobactam** (best single agent) | 4.5 g IV q6h | 4-7 days post-drainage |
| **Ceftriaxone + metronidazole** (if pen allergic) | Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily + Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h | Same |
| **Meropenem** (for severe/high-resistance) | 1g IV q8h | Tailor to cultures |

- **Decision:** Call senior / gastro / interventional radiology to arrange **urgent biliary drainage** (ideally ERCP within 24 hours). For severe cholangitis (shock, confusion, high lactate), aim for <12 hours.
- **What NOT to do:** Do not just "monitor" or "wait for morning" – obstruction must be relieved. Do not give only antibiotics and discharge.

**Ward (24-72h) – "Drain, Monitor, Source Control"**

- **ERCP** with sphincterotomy, stone extraction, or stent placement. If ERCP impossible (altered anatomy, coagulopathy) → **percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage (PTBD)** by interventional radiology.
- **Monitor:** NEWS2 q1h until stable, then q4h. Daily LFTs, FBC, CRP. Watch for post-procedure pancreatitis, bleeding, perforation.
- **Adjust antibiotics** based on bile culture results (when available, 48-72h). De-escalate if possible.
- **Nutrition:** Nil by mouth until drainage established. Then advance as tolerated – no low-fat diet evidence; just avoid further obstruction.

**Long-term – "Prevent Recurrence"**

- **Cholecystectomy** – for gallbladder-in-situ patients, perform laparoscopic cholecystectomy after recovery (typically 4-6 weeks post-drainage). Do not do it during acute episode (higher risk of conversion, infection).
- **Treat underlying cause:** If stricture – consider balloon dilation, stent exchange. If malignant – refer to hepatobiliary oncology.
- **Consider ursodeoxycholic acid** for recurrent cholangitis in PSC (off-label use, no strong evidence).
- **What NOT to do:** Don’t forget to schedule the cholecystectomy – recurrence rate without it is 30-50% at 1 year.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Acute Cholangitis | Acute Cholecystitis | Acute Pancreatitis | Hepatic Abscess |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Pain** | RUQ, dull, constant ± back | RUQ, crescendo, may radiate to right shoulder | Epigastric, bores through to back | RUQ, constant, may be pleuritic |
| **Exam** | RUQ tenderness, no guarding unless empyema | Murphy’s sign (inspiratory arrest), mass | Epigastric guarding, Grey Turner/Cullen (late) | Hepatomegaly, tender liver |
| **Jaundice** | Present in >80% (obstructive) | Variable (mild in ~10% if Mirizzi) | Early mild jaundice, check direct vs indirect | Uncommon unless abscess compresses ducts |
| **Fever** | High, with rigors | Low-grade, no rigors usually | Mild/no fever unless infected necrosis | High, swinging |
| **LFTs** | Obstructive ↑ALP, ↑GGT, bilirubin; ALT mildly ↑ | Normal or mild ↑ALT | Moderate ↑AST/ALT (2-5x) + ↑lipase >3x ULN | Cholestatic if adjacent bile ducts compressed |
| **Key imaging** | Dilated bile duct, stone/stricture | Thickened gallbladder wall, pericholecystic fluid | Oedematous pancreas, necrosis | Single/multiple fluid collections ± gas in abscess |
| **Key differ** | Pus in bile at ERCP | Positive Murphy sign + GB wall thickening | Lipase >3x ULN | CT/MRI shows abscess cavity |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Septic shock** | Hypotension resistant to fluids, increasing lactate >2, altered GCS | NEWS2 ≥7, lactate >4, oliguria | Escalate to ICU, start vasopressors (norepinephrine), goal MAP ≥65, reassess for urgent drainage within hours |
| **Biliary peritonitis / gallbladder empyema** | RUQ rigidity, guarding, rebound; fever high | Tender mass, positive psoas sign | Urgent CT, emergency drainage (ERCP or PTBD) + surgery if perforated |
| **Liver abscess** | High swinging fevers, right upper quadrant pain, referred pain to shoulder | Hepatomegaly, ↑ALP, iron deficiency anaemia | Long course antibiotics (4-6 weeks), percutaneous drainage if >5 cm |
| **Acute kidney injury** | Oliguria, rising creatinine | Sepsis-induced hypoperfusion + nephrotoxic drugs | Fluids, avoid NSAIDs, remove obstructive cause |
| **Pancreatitis** | Epigastric pain, vomiting, elevated amylase/lipase | Ductal obstruction (e.g., stone in ampulla) | Endoscopic stone extraction, supportive care |
| **Late: Secondary biliary cirrhosis** | Insidious: ascites, splenomegaly, coagulopathy from chronic obstruction | Prolonged untreated obstruction (e.g., benign stricture) | Surgical drainage, liver transplant assessment |

**Late-presentation pattern – "the neglected cholangitis":**  
A patient presents with ascites, jaundice, and a rock-hard liver; no fever. They were jaundiced for months after a cholecystectomy and never followed up. The "missed" complication is **secondary biliary cirrhosis** from a retained stricture – irreversible unless caught early.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **Charcot’s Triad:** *“Pain, Fever, Jaundice”* – remember that you only need two for clinical suspicion.
2. **Reynolds’ Pentad:** *“Charcot + Shock + Confusion”* – for the septic variant needing immediate ICU.
3. **TG18 Criteria:** *“C-holestasis + I-nflammation + I-maging”* – (C+I+I) = diagnosis.

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

- **“Pus under pressure”** – the core concept. If you drain it, they get better; if you don't, they die. Antibiotics alone are inadequate.
- **Post-cholecystectomy sepsis** – always think cholangitis from a retained stone. Get an MRCP.
- **Bilirubin >50 μmol/L + dilated CBD >10mm** = almost certainly an obstructing stone. No further delays – book ERCP.
- **Fever + RUQ pain + normal ultrasound** – consider early infection without duct dilatation (e.g., segmental cholangitis in PSC). Still need MRCP/ERCP.
- **Monitor for post-ERCP pancreatitis** – especially in young female after sphincterotomy. If pain recurs within 6h, check amylase.
- **Blood cultures positive for *E. coli* or *Klebsiella* + hyperbilirubinaemia** – this is cholangitis until proven otherwise.
- **Never forget the gallbladder** – even after a successful ERCP, if the gallbladder is still in situ, plan cholecystectomy within 6 weeks to prevent recurrent obstruction.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**

| Mistake | Correct approach |
|---------|------------------|
| Starting antibiotics but not arranging drainage | Drainage is definitive therapy – antibiotics buy time |
| Using only Gram-negative coverage (e.g., ciprofloxacin alone) | Need Gram-negative, Gram-positive (enterococcus), and anaerobic coverage |
| Failing to get blood cultures before antibiotics | 60% yield; if negative, you can't narrow therapy |
| Doing a cholecystectomy during acute cholangitis | Wait 4-6 weeks – high risk of complications |

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call your senior (registrar / on-call consultant):**

- Any patient with **Charcot’s triad** (even without shock) – needs drainage decision.
- **NEWS2 ≥5** (any single orange score OR sum total).
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** – start early goal-directed therapy.
- **GCS <15** (particularly drowsiness or confusion) – indicates severe cholangitis.
- **Systolic BP <90 mmHg** despite 1L crystalloid – suspect septic shock.
- **Oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/h for >2h)** – risk of AKI.

**When to call ICU / crash team:**

- **NEWS2 ≥7** (or any red score as per local policy).
- **Requiring vasopressors** (IV fluids insufficient to maintain MAP ≥65).
- **GCS <9** or new focal neurology.
- **Rising lactate >4 mmol/L** despite initial resuscitation.
- **Progressive respiratory failure** (RR >30, PaO2 <8 kPa on O2).
- **Septic shock with multi-organ failure** (renal, hepatic, respiratory).

**Specific thresholds – use NEWS2 as your safety net:**
- NEWS2 0-4: Stable, plan ward management
- NEWS2 5-6: Immediate senior review, consider HDU/step-up
- NEWS2 ≥7: **Crash call / ICU outreach** – this patient requires organ support

**Bottom line:** If you even suspect severe acute cholangitis, **call early**, **start antibiotics**, **arrange drainage**. Do not watch a septic biliary patient deteriorate overnight. The one-shot teaching: **"Pus under pressure kills fast – drain it to save them."**
